federal_presidential_consitiutional_repu: The government of the United States can be described as a Federal Presidential Constitutional Republic; that is, power is shared in a %&federal system%/&federal_system, a %&president%/&president serves as the head of state, laws for the government are given in the %&Constitution%/&constitution, and political power is conferred through %&public representatives%/&congress.
federal_system: The federal system describes the seperation of powers within a nation between 'levels' of government. In the United States, soverignty is shared between the Federal, State, and Local levels of government.
state: A state (or commonwealth) is one of 50 such political entities within the United States of America. Each state has jurisdiction over a set geographic area and sets many of their own laws and policies.
party: Though not described in the &Constitution&constitution, political parties have come to hold tremendous political power in the United States. Political parties are formed by like-minded &political constituents&voters to represent at-large a given &ideology&ideology and/or &political goal&issue.
convention: The convention process is designed to determine which &candidates&candidate are most apt to represent their &political parties&party in the &general election&general_election.
primary: Primary elections are a part of the &candidate-selection system&convention. A popular vote amongst the &voting public&voters determines the preferred candidate through secret ballots. &Delegates&delegate are awarded to favored candidates.
closed_primary: A closed primary is a &primary&primary in which only voters registered with a particular &party&party may participate. In some cases, &independent voters&independent are allowed to participate.
semi_closed_primary: A semi-closed or open partisan primary is a &primary&primary in which &unaffiliated&independent voters may vote for either party, but cross-party voting is prohibited.
open primary: An open primary is a &primary&primary in which voters may vote for any one candidate of their choice. In some cases, cross-voters may be required to declare their choice publically.
caucus: Caucuses are a part of the &candidate-selection system&convention. Participants often debate about the candidates and publically declare their preferred candidate. %Delegates&delegate are awarded to favored candidates.
delegate: Delegates are people who represent their &state&state at a &party's&party &nomination convention&convention. Delegates are selected by the results of a &primary&primary or &caucus&caucus, or may be given the position in recognition of their party leadership.
front_loading: &States&state often attempt to 'front-load' their %primaries%primary or &caucuses&caucus by pushing them as early as possible in the year. This is done to attempt to gain influence in the course of the election and on party decisions. &Parties&party combat this by offering &delegate&delegate-count incentives for later dates.
bloc: A voting bloc is a group of &voters&voters distinguised by a &demographic trait&demographic or by a preference on a given &political issue&issue. Candidates often focus on gaining support of a bloc rather than focusing on individual voters, simplifying the &campaign process&campaign.
demographic: A demographic is a grouped sector of a population with some common inherent feature like &age&generation, &race or ethnicity&race_ethnicity, &religion&religion, etc. &Demographic blocs&bloc are often used to analyze trends in population data.
generation: A common &demographic classification&demographic has to do with age. People born around a certain time (usually about 20-30 years apart) are assigned to a Generational Cohort. People in different generations are likely to have had a different experience of childhood and adulthood.
race_ethnicity: A common &demographic classification&demographic has to do with race or ethnicity. Since the 17th century, 'race' has been used to group persons with a kinship relation into one bloc. Ethnicity has to do with common nations of origin, ancestry, tradition, etc.
religion: Religion describes a societal-cultural system of belief, morality, worldview, and faith relating humanity to elements and interpretations of the observable world. For most people, religion constitutes a lens through which to understand and interpret the meaning and purpose of life and everyday existence.
family_status: Family status describes the general condiditon of one's home life. Namedly, one being single or married can evidence a great difference in one's day-to-day life, as can the number of children in a household.
education: Education describes the maximum academic achievement of an individual. In the United States, formal education is divided into pre-primary (nursery schools and Kindergartens), elementary grades (primary school), middle or junior-high grade school, high or senior-high school, tertiary / undergraduate education, masters or professional studies, and doctoral studies.
employment: Employment is an important factor of the lives of many Americans. As a demographic bloc, employment is used to distinguish between industries or classes of work.
residency: Residency is a term used to describe the conditions of one's home location and surrounding geography. Residents of urban areas live within densely-populated areas like cities. Residents of sub-urban areas live in somewhat less dense areas, though there are often major commercial areas within suburbs. Residents of rural areas live outside towns or cities marked by low population density.
voter: A voter is a constituent of the United States government who may legally participate in local, state, and federal elections. Nearly all registered US citizens over the age of 18 are allowed to vote, about 75% of the total population. Because all other people are non-voters, political candidates often have little reason to appeal to constituents who are not a part of this bloc.
constitution: The Constitution is the &supreme law&law of the United States. It outlines such government systems as the &Federal System&federal_system, &Separation of Powers&separation_of_powers, and the broad political processes and duties of government offices. The Consitution was ratified in 1787, and is considered the most important political document in the United States.
bill_of_rights: The Bill of Rights is a collective name for the first ten &Amendments&amendments to the &Constitution&constitution. The early Anti-Federalist party ensured that the document establishes clear limitations on government power and guarantees of personal rights and freedoms.
amendments: Amendments to the &Constitution&constitution are changes which serve to clarify definitions, add rights and liberties, change processes, and prohibit certain actions. Amendments are proposed by &Congress&congress and ratified by the &State Governments&.
law: The law consists of regulations and codes for residents of the USA, &levels of government&federal_system, and &judicial processes&court related to certain actions, processes, and rights. The %Constitution&constitution and &Bill of Rights&bill_of_rights ensure that State laws take precedence over Federal ones.
policy: Policy is a broad political term which describes the adopted or proposed actions of a group.
executive_order: An executive order is a directive issued by the &President&president.
president: The President of the United States is the Chief &Executive&executive_branch Officer of the nation. The President is elected every four years by the Electoral College, and one person may serve at most two terms (as per the &22nd Amendment&amendments).
vice_president: The Vice President is the second officer of the &Executive Branch&executive_branch. The Vice President also serves as the President of the &Senate&senate and is responsible for breaking tied votes in that chamber. The Vice President is elected along with the President (as per the &12th Amendment&amendments).
speaker_of_the_house: The Speaker of the House of Representatives is the presiding officer of the &House of Representatives&house_of_representatives. By tradition, the Speaker is the head of the majority &party&party in the House.
cabinet: The Cabinet of the United States is an advisory council to the &President&president. Cabinet members are the heads of their respective &Departments&department and are appointed by the President and confirmed by the &Senate&senate. There are 26 members of the Cabinet.
attorney_general: The Attorney General serves as the head of the &Department of Justice&department_of_justice and is a member of the &Cabinet&cabinet.
department_of_justice: The Department of Justice is responsible for domestic enforcement of federal laws, and the internal administration of justice.
secretary_of_state: The Secretary of State is the president's chief foreign affairs advisor, carrying out foreign policy through the Foreign Service, Civil Service, and Agency for International Development.
department: In the Executive Branch of the Federal Government, the departments are the principle units of executive administration. They are each directed by a secretary appointed by the President.
secretary: In the Federal Government, a secretary is an appointed member of the President's cabinet who serves as the leader of a cabinet agency.
white_house: The White House is the official residence and workplace of the President of the United States. Symbolically, the White House is used to refer to the Executive Branch or even the Federal Government as a whole.
election: Elections are the primary way that &common voters&voters influence the government. At each &level of government&federal_system, citizens make their preferences known through popular elections.
local_government: The local government is the smallest level of the federal system, consisting of county- or city-level officials.
county: Counties are an administrative division within States, each of which have some kind of local government.
parish: In Louisiana, County-equivalent subdivisions are called Parishes.
borough: In Alaska, organized County-equivalent subdivisions are called Boroughs.
unorganized_borough: Not all the land in Alaska is organized into a Borough. The Unorganized Borough has no local government except for some school districts and tribal villages. To aid in the Census, the Unorganized Borough is divided into 11 Census Areas.
census_area_alaska: The large Unorganized Borough of Alaska is not divided into County-equivalent subdivisions, but is covered instead by 11 Census Areas for the purposes of census-taking.
municipality: Municipalities are various types of inhabited places in the United States. This is the broadest term used to describe Cities, Villages, Towns, and other kinds of Census-Designated populated areas.
city: In most states, Cities are the largest designated Municipal incorporation. Cities are usually areas of denser population and have their own local government.
congress: Congress is the legislative body at the federal level, consisting of the House of Representatives and the Senate. In the Constitution, Congress was given the power to create new laws, oversee taxes, and enter into diplomacy with other governments.
senate: The Senate is the upper chamber of &Congress&congress. It consists of 100 &Senators&senator, two from each &state&state. The &Vice President&vice_president is also the President of the Senate.
house_of_representatives: The House of Representatives is the lower chamber of &Congress&congress. It consists of an apportioned number of Representatives (exactly 435 since 1911) according to the populations of the &states&state. The &Speaker of the House&speaker_of_the_house presides over the House.
congressional_district: Each Representative member of the House of Representatives is elected by their Congressional District. The methods of drawing Districts is chosen and administered by each State, meaning there are many different laws and rules governing the process.
supreme_court: The Supreme Court is the highest court in the nation and represents the Judicial Branch at the Federal level. It consists of several Justices of the Supreme Court, one of whom is Chief Justice. The Court determines the constitutionality of laws through Judicial Review, and hears new cases to determine or redefine legal precedent.
electoral_college: The electoral college is the unique system of American elections by which the President is actually chosen. When voters cast their ballot, they are actually selecting an Elector, who will attend the Electoral College in their state to vote for a presidential and vice presidential candidate. The candidate who recieves more than half of the electors' votes will become the next President.
apportionment: Apportionment is the process by which the House of Representatives (and therefore the Electoral College) maintains its formal representation of the population of states. More populous states recieve more Representative seats, and less populous states recieve fewer. Reapportionment is determined based on the Census, which occurs every decade across the Nation.
separation_of_powers: The Separation of Powers is a key goal of the Constitutionally-framed federal government system. This idea requires that no single body holds total power over the nation, leading to the systems of Governmental Branches, Checks and Balances, and Bicamery.
executive_branch: The Executive &Branch of Government&separation_of_powers is officially responsible for enforcing the &laws&law of the United States. At the &Federal&federal_system level, it consists of the &President&president, the &Vice President&vice_president, and the &Cabinet&cabinet and their associated Departments. Article II of the &Constitution&constitution describes the structure and role of the Executive Branch.
legislative_branch: The Legislative &Branch of Government&separation_of_powers is responsible for creating the &law&law of the United States. At the &Federal&federal_system level, it consists of &Congress&congress and its members. The &Vice President&vice_president and &Speaker of the House&speaker_of_the_house are the most powerful officials in this branch. Article I of the &Constitution&constitution describes the structure and duties of the Legislative Branch.
judicial_branch: The Judicial &Branch of Government&separation_of_powers is responsible for interpreting the &law&law of the United States. It consists of all &Courts&court in the United States, from District or Municipal Courts at the local level to the Supreme Court, which serves as the highest court in the nation. Article III of the &Constitution&constitution describes the function and processes of the Judicial Branch.
court: A court is a judicial body which administers justice by resolving legal and civil disputes. There are many types of courts throughout the federal system, but the Supreme Court is the chief among them.
alabama: The State of Alabama is located in the Deep South, and borders Tennessee, Georgia, Florida, and Mississippi. Alabama was admitted on the 14th of December, 1819, as the 22nd state in the Union. Alabama is notable for its diverse geography. Alabama has the longest written constituon in the world, composed of around 370 thousand words. Alabama is predominantly protestant, but with mixed demographics. Alabama is strongly conservative.
alaska: The State of Alaska is the northernmost state of the United States, and is one of the two non-contiguous states. Alaska was admitted on the 3rd of January, 1959, as the 49th state in the Union. Alaska is notable as the largest U.S. state by area, comprising of more total area than the next three states combined. Alaska alone contributes more than half of the coastline of the United States, and also has more than 3 million lakes. Alaska is sparsely populated, with a notable Native Indian population. On the national stage, Alaska is generally conservative, though state party control is more mixed.
arizona: The State of Arizona is in the Southwest, being one of the Four Corners states and bordering Colorado, New Mexico, Utah, Nevada, and California, as well as an international border with Mexico. Arizona was admitted on the 14th of February, 1912, the last of the 48 contiguous states to be admitted to the Union. Arizona is known for its desert climate, with an extremely hot climate in the south of the state. Arizona has a diverse population, with around a quarter of the state being Indian Reservation land. Arizona is considered a swing state.
arkansas: The State of Arkansas is in the West South Central region, bordering Missouri, Tennessee, Mississippi, Louisiana, Texas, and Oklahoma. Arkansas was admitted on the 15th of June, 1836, as the 25th state. Arkansas is the only state that produces diamonds. The Walmart Corporation is headquartered in Arkansas. Arkansas is primarily white and protestant, and is strongly conservative.
california: The State of California is a Pacific state, with much of the west coast of the continental United States being in California. Oregon, Nevada, and Arizona border California, and the state shares a border with Mexico in the south. California was admitted on September 9th, 1850 as the 31st state. California is known for its dominant media industry, especially Hollywood, its innovative tech companies in Silicon Valley, and its vast natural beauty. California is the most populous state, with major cities like Los Angeles, San Francisco, and San Diego being home to more than 25 million people. These urban clusters make California one of the most liberal states.
district_of_columbia: The District of Columbia is the capital district of the United States, in which the national capital city of Washington, D.C. is located. Though it is not a state, the District of Columbia is incorportated under the Constitution and falls under the direct jurisdiction of Congress, recieving 3 electoral votes in the Electoral College. The District of Columbia is plurality African American, and is overwhelmingly Democratic-voting.
new_years_day: New Year's Day is the first day of the new year, January 1st. Some people make resolutions for habits or lifestyle changes for the next year.
epiphany: Epiphany, Three Kings Day, or Little Christmas is a Christian feast commemorating the visit of the Magi to the Christ Child, celebrated January 6th. Customs include singing, blessing the home, eating cake, and church services.
orthodox_christmas: Christmas is celebrated on January 7th by Eastern Christians.
mlk_day: Martin Luther King, Jr. Day remembers King's contributions to civil rights and Black equality in the United States through service projects and events on the third Monday in January.
inauguration_day: On January 20th every four years, the president-elect takes the oath of office and delivers an inauguration speech.
lunar_new_year: In many East Asian countries, the Lunar New Year is the beginning of the calendar. Chinese New Year is a celebration of family and fortune, with the color red symbolizing good luck.
ramadan: Ramadan is the ninth month of the Islamic calendar, observed as a time of fasting from sunup to sundown, communal prayer, and spiritual focus.
groundhog_day: Groundhog Day on February 2nd is a Dutch folklore holiday predicting the arrival of Spring. If a groundhog sees its shadow, six more weeks of winter will follow; otherwise, spring will arive early. The most famous groundhog is Punxsutawney Phil in Pennsylvania.
mardi_gras: Mardi Gras, also called Shrove Tuesday or Fat Tuesday, is the final day of Carnival and the day before Ash Wednesday. Cities with French heritage along the Gulf Coast have late-night festivals and balls involving costumes, music, food, and drink.
ash_wednesday: Ash Wednesday is the first day of Lent, observed by prayer, fasting, and almsgiving. Many Lent observers fast from red meat during the seven weeks from Ash Wednesday to Easter Sunday.
valentines_day: Valentine's Day celebrates love and affection on February 14th, including exchanging cards, flowers, and chocolates to one's Valentine or loved ones.
presidents_day: Presidents' Day on the third Monday in February honors U.S. presidents, especially Washington and Lincoln.
eid_al_fitr: Eid al-Fitr is the end of Ramadan and the first main festival of Islam, breaking the fast. Feasts, prayer, and charitous giving are some ways to celebrate.
holi: Holi is the Hindu festival of colors in celebration of springtime and love. Colored powders are thrown at dances to spread color to celebrators.
st_patricks_day: St. Patrick's Day honors Irish heritage on March 17th. Traditions include wearing green, attending parades, having festive drinks, hunting leprechauns, and listening to Irish music.
palm_sunday: Palm Sunday is a Christian feast on the Sunday before Easter. The feast commemorates Jesus's entry into Jerusalem, with palm branches being waved by the crowd in honor. Palm Sunday is the first day of Holy Week.
good_friday: The Friday before Easter is called Good Friday, a Christian commemoration of the crucifixion of Jesus. Church services and fasting are observed.
navratri: Navratri or Navaratri is a Hindu festival honoring the goddess Durga, spanning nine nights. Recitations and decorations are ways to celebrate.
easter: Easter is a Christian holiday celebrating the 3rd-day resurrection of Jesus. Many Americans attend church services, go hunting for painted eggs, tell stories of the Easter Bunny, and have festive meals.
april_fools_day: April Fool's Day on the first day of April is a day of humor and playful pranks, tricks, and jokes.
passover: Passover is a Jewish holiday commemorating the Exodus of the Israelites from captivity in Egypt. Ritual feasts without leavened bread are eaten in rememberance.
tax_day: Tax day is the April 15th deadline to file the U.S. federal income tax.
earth_day: Earth Day promotes eco-consciousness on April 22nd, including cleaning up parks, planting trees, and learning about the planet.
arbor_day: Arbor Day celebrates trees and nature, including planting trees and conservation efforts on the last Friday in April.
may_day: May Day is a celebration of workers, and of the start of spring. Some places have dances and festivals, while workers' activism is the focus in other areas.
eid_al_adha: Eid al-Adha is the second main festival of Islam, commemorating Abraham's devotion. The festival involves communal prayer, wearing new clothes, and eating meat.
cinco_de_mayo: Cinco de Mayo (the 5th of May) commemorates Mexico's victory over France in the Battle of Puebla 1862. A celebration of Hispanic culture, Americans have parades, listen to Latin music, and eat Mexican foods.
ascension: The Feast of the Ascension (also Solemnity or Holy Thursday) is a Christian commemoration of the ascension of Jesus into Heaven. It marks the beginning of Ascensiontide lasting until the Feast of Pentecost.
vesak: Vesak (Buddha Day) is a holiday observed by Buddhists on the birth, enlightenment, and death of Gautama Buddha. Among the most important Buddhist festivals, celebration involves community singing, releasing and caring for animals, and recitation.
mothers_day: Mother's Day on the second Sunday in May honors mothers by giving cards, flowers, and having a Sunday meal with family.
pentecost: Pentecost (also Whit Sunday) is a Christian holiday commemorating the Holy Spirit's descent to the Apostles of Christ. Observence involves prayer, decorating in red, and singing.
armed_forces_day: Armed Forces Day is a day dedicated to honoring active military personnel. Official ceremonies, parades, or flyovers may be observed.
memorial_day: Memorial Day is observed on the last Monday in May, honoring U.S. military personnel who died in service. Ceremonies, parades, and cemetary speeches are common.
ashura: Ashura is an Islamic day of commemoration, marking several auspicious traditional events involving Adam, Noah, Joseph, and Moses. Celebration for Sunni Muslims involves fasting, carnivals, bonfires; for Shia, mourning Husayn ibn Ali.
flag_day: Flag Day on June 14th commemorates adoption of the U.S. flagin 1777. Displays and ceremonies involving the flag remember this history.
fathers_day: Father's Day on the third Sunday in June honors fathers, including gifts, lunches, and family outings.
juneteenth: Juneteenth commemorates the emancipation of enslaved African Americans in 1865. June 19th parades and festivals promote reflection on the heritage of the nation.
pride: Pride Month spans the month of June, celebrating LGBTQ culture and community. The anniversary of the 1969 Stonewall riots and liberation protests is June 28th.
independence_day: Independence Day or the 4th of July celebrates the Declaration of Independence being publically read in 1776. Americans celebrate with fireworks, barbecues, and patriotic parades.
mawlid: Mawlid is an Islamic festival commemorating the birth of the prophet Muhammad. Religious poetry, fasting, music, and prayer are done in recognition.
ganesh_chaturthi: Ganesh Chaturthi is a Hindu festival celebrating the diety Ganesha. Murtis or icons of the diety are installed in homes, and offerings and prayers are offered. It is considered a day of new beginnings and wisdom.
labor_day: Labor Day recognizes American workers on the first Monday in September. Parades and picnics mark the unofficial end of summer.
patriot_day: Patriot Day remembers the victims of the September 11th attacks, observed with moments of silence and memorial services.
constitution_day: Constitution Day (Citizenship Day, I Am an American Day) observes the signing and adoption of the Constitution on September 17th, 1787. Schools and institutions provide education on the history of the Constitution.
rosh_hashanah: Rosh Hashanah is the Jewish New Year, marked by prayers, blowing the shofar, and eating foods like apples with honey.
yom_kippur: Yom Kippur is the Jewish day of atonement, observed with fasting, prayer, and inner reflection.
columbus_day: Columbus Day on the second Monday in October marks the 1492 arrival of Christopher Columbus to the new world.
indigenous_peoples_day: Native American heritage is observed with cultural events and education on the second Monday in October.
diwali: Diwali is the Hindu festival of lights celebrating the victory of light over darkness. Feasts and sweets, lighting lamps, and making intricate Rangoli patterns are traditions.
halloween: Halloween is a day of spooky fun and folklore on October 31st. Celebrators wear costumes and walk door-to-door trick-or-treating. Haunted houses and games like apple-bobbing are also ways to celebrate.
day_of_the_dead: The Day of the Dead (Día de los Muertos) is celebrated in areas with strong Hispanic heritage. Ancestors, life, death, peace, and family are celebrated with parades, cakes, Ofrenda altars, and skull decorations.
all_saints_day: All Saints' Day (All Hallows' Day) is a celebration honoring Christian saints and martyrs on November 1st. Vigils, special Mass, and feasts remember ancestors and dead members of the faith.
election_day: Election Day is the Tuesday after the first Monday in November. U.S. federal elections call voting Americans to the polls to participate in the electoral process.
veterans_day: Veterans Day, also called Armistice Day, honors all veterans of the U.S. military with ceremonies, parades, and moments of silence on November 11th.
thanksgiving: Thanksgiving is a day of giving thanks, celebrated on the fourth Thursday in November. This day of gratitude marks the end of the harvest season as families gather to share a turkey dinner and watch parades and football.
black_friday: Black Friday is the day after Thanksgiving, when shops have huge doorbuster discounts for the start of the holiday season.
small_business_saturday: On Small Business Saturday following Black Friday, local businesses have holiday sales.
cyber_monday: Cyber Monday, the week after Thanksgiving, is an online shopping holiday when e-commerce sites have digital deals.
pearl_harbor_day: Pearl Harbor Day remembers the December 7 attack on Pearl Harbor in 1941, when the U.S. entered World War II.
immaculate_conception: The Feast of the Immaculate Conception is a Catholic feast recalling the doctrine of Mary's perpetual virginity.
hanukkah: Hanukkah (or Chanukah) is the Jewish festival of lights. Traditions include lighting the menorah, playing dreidel games, and eating fried foods.
christmas_eve: Chistmas Eve is the day before Christmas, December 24th. Many churches have a candlelit nighttime service.
christmas_day: Christmas is a Christian holiday celebrating the birth of Jesus, observed mostly on December 25th. Families and friends exchange gifts under a decorated Christmas tree, have meals together, and tell stories of Santa Claus.
kwanzaa: Kwanzaa is an African American cultural holiday celebrating heritage and unity. Stories are told around colored candles.
new_years_eve: New Year's Eve is December 31st, the last day of the year. People gather to see the year out with parties, fireworks, and to watch the ball drop broadcasted from New York City.
